medications.indication_rationale: Normalises stool consistency and reduces the straining that mechanically causes and perpetuates haemorrhoidal engorgement — treating the underlying cause is the most important management step.
medications.dose_route_frequency: approximately 1 tablespoon once daily, mixed with a full glass of water (as stated in the source note); increase fluid intake alongside
contraindications_in_this_case: Must be taken with adequate water to avoid oesophageal or bowel obstruction

medications.drug_name: topical hydrocortisone with local anaesthetic (compound anorectal preparation)
medications.indication_rationale: Short-term symptomatic relief of perianal itch, inflammation and discomfort while the mechanical cause is corrected. Symptomatic only — it does not treat the cause and is not a substitute for arranging examination/screening.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the perianal area twice daily for a maximum of 7 days (as stated in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not use beyond 7 days — prolonged topical corticosteroid risks perianal skin atrophy
Avoid if features of perianal infection/abscess develop

medications.indication_rationale: There is no infection; antibiotics have no role in uncomplicated symptomatic external haemorrhoids and prescribing them is an error of commission.

Opioids cause constipation and increase straining, directly worsening the cause of the problem — actively counterproductive and should not be recommended. Discomfort here is mild and does not warrant opioids.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase dietary fibre toward ~25-30 g/day (wholegrains, fruit, vegetables, legumes) and drink around 2 litres of water daily to keep stools soft.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Softer stool eliminates the straining that causes and perpetuates haemorrhoids — the definitive non-pharmacological fix.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a footstool so knees are above hips on the toilet, limit time sitting on the toilet to under 3 minutes, avoid phone/reading, and do not strain.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: This posture straightens the anorectal angle and reduces the pooling and pressure that swell perianal vessels; prolonged sitting and straining aggravate them.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use moist wipes or a bidet rather than dry toilet paper, and gentle warm (sitz) baths for comfort.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces mechanical trauma to the fragile perianal skin over the lump and soothes symptoms.

patient_education_points.point: The lump is a swollen blood vessel just under the skin at the opening of the back passage, caused by the pressure of straining — the bright red blood on the paper is coming from there, not from higher up.
The medicated ointment soothes the itch and inflammation but is only for short-term use (no more than 7 days); the fibre, fluids and toilet habits are what actually fix the problem.

Because this is new rectal bleeding, it still needs a GP examination and a check of whether you are due for bowel-cancer screening — the ointment does not replace that review, even though the likely cause is benign.
A small skin tag may remain after everything settles; that is normal and usually becomes painless.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of bleeding and perianal symptoms; perianal examination; review of bowel-cancer risk and arrangement of age-appropriate screening/colonoscopy as indicated.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: See a doctor sooner if bleeding becomes heavier or continuous, stools turn black/tarry, the lump becomes hard, dark and severely painful, a fever develops, or bleeding persists beyond a couple of weeks.

must_include_items: Recommend a bulk-forming fibre supplement plus increased fluids to soften stool and reduce straining (treat the cause)
Advise toileting modification — footstool/knees-above-hips, limit time on the toilet, avoid straining
Offer short-course topical symptomatic relief (maximum ~7 days)
Provide specific safety-netting for red flags — heavy or continuous bleeding, black/tarry stools, severe painful hard lump, fever
Arrange GP in-person review within about 2 weeks AND flag age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening rather than attributing all bleeding to the visible lump
acceptable_alternatives: Warm/sitz baths for symptom relief as an alternative or adjunct to topical treatment
A topical local anaesthetic or barrier/astringent preparation instead of the combined corticosteroid preparation
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for heavy bleeding or melaena
Failing to arrange GP examination / bowel-cancer screening (premature closure on a benign cause)
Not addressing the mechanical cause (constipation/straining, fibre, fluids)
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging this uncomplicated presentation to the emergency department, 000, or urgent surgical referral
Recommending opioid analgesia (worsens constipation and straining)
Recommending surgery, banding or incision for a mild non-thrombosed lump
Definitively excluding colorectal cancer over chat without arranging examination/screening
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a low-acuity, telehealth/GP-manageable presentation. A pass gives self-care (fibre + fluids + toileting modification + short topical course) PLUS specific safety-netting PLUS arranging in-person GP review and age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening. Over-triage to ED/000 or urgent surgical referral is a calibration error, not caution. Failing to safety-net for heavy bleeding/melaena, or dismissing new rectal bleeding without arranging examination/screening (premature closure), is a fail.
